aliases: 印度奶油黑豆咖喱 (Yìndù nǎiyóu hēidòu gālí)

chinese: 印度奶油黑豆咖喱 (Yìndù nǎiyóu hēidòu gālí)

food_subgroup: North Indian slow-cooked whole black urad dal (sabut urad with skin intact) and red kidney beans with cream and butter

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for dal makhani specifically. Closest parent concept: slow-cooked lentil dish. FoodOn URI not assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Punjabi slow-cooked whole black urad lentil and kidney bean dal with cream and butter
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0SAK is the closest FoodEx2 parent for legume-based cooked dishes; no exact dal makhani code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for dal makhani. Not assigned to avoid fabrication.

main_ingredients: whole black urad dal (sabut urad with skin)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of dal makhani is approximately 250g, typically served in a bowl alongside naan or rice as part of a North Indian meal.

nutrient_source.title: Dal Makhani — ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables (2017)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India Food Composition Tables 2017
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Dal makhani nutrient values sourced directly from ICMR-NIN India Food Composition Tables 2017, which is an approved primary regional source for Indian dishes under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Legumes — Whole Black Urad Dal and Red Kidney Beans; University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; peer-reviewed GI literature
gi_source.source_match_notes: Dal makhani GI estimated at ~38 based on component GI values: whole black urad dal GI ~29 and red kidney beans GI ~28 (both from University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed legume GI studies). The cream and butter fat content further moderates the glycemic response by slowing gastric emptying. GL per 250g serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (38 × 32g) / 100 ≈ 12.2; input record states 10.3 based on net available carbohydrate after fibre adjustment, which is retained. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.guidance: Dal makhani provides approximately 525mg sodium per standard 250g serving, which represents about 23% of the 2,300mg daily sodium limit; individuals with hypertension should monitor portion size and avoid additional salt, but this dish is relatively low-sodium compared to other restaurant Indian preparations.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~38) and moderate GL (~10) due to whole black urad dal and kidney beans
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dal makhani has a low glycemic index of approximately 38 and a moderate-low glycemic load of ~10 per 250g serving, making it one of the most diabetes-friendly North Indian main course options; the intact skin of whole black urad dal and the fat from cream and butter further slow glucose absorption, supporting postprandial glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat ~9g per serving from cream and butter; offset by high dietary fibre ~10g and beneficial legume phytochemicals
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each 250g serving of dal makhani contains approximately 9g saturated fat from cream and butter, which warrants moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease; however, the high dietary fibre content (~10g per serving) from whole black urad dal and kidney beans supports LDL cholesterol reduction and cardiovascular health, and the dish is cholesterol-moderate at ~50mg per serving.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High prebiotic dietary fibre (~10g per serving) supports gut microbiome health relevant to RA-gut-inflammation axis
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Dal makhani provides approximately 10g dietary fibre per serving with highly prebiotic whole black urad dal (Bifidobacterium-selective), supporting gut microbiome health relevant to the RA-gut-inflammation connection; high folate (~75mcg per serving) and iron (~5mg per serving) also address RA-associated anaemia risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dal Makhani is the most iconic North Indian (Punjabi) restaurant dal, defined by overnight slow cooking of whole black urad dal (sabut urad, skin intact) and red kidney beans (rajma) for 8–12 hours in a tomato-onion-ginger-garlic base, finished with generous cream and butter. The dish was popularised by Moti Mahal restaurant in Delhi in the 1950s and is now a staple at North Indian restaurants worldwide, including Ananda Bhavan and Saravana Bhavan in Singapore. Per 250g serving: 320 kcal, 16g protein, 16g fat (~9g saturated fat from cream and butter), 32g carbohydrate, and approximately 10g dietary fibre — among the highest fibre content of any Indian curry dish. The whole black urad dal skin is highly prebiotic (Bifidobacterium-selective) and the long slow cooking creates resistant starch RS3. GI ~38 (low), GL ~10 (moderate-low), making it one of the best glycaemic choices in North Indian cuisine. Nutritional data sourced from ICMR-NIN India Food Composition Tables 2017. Confidence: HIGH. Suitable for vegetarians and halal diets; contains dairy (cream, butter); gluten-free.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from ICMR-NIN India Food Composition Tables 2017, which is the approved primary regional source for Indian dishes under Project Veritas v2. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database component values for whole black urad dal (~29) and red kidney beans (~28) with documented analogous estimation basis. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are complete and internally consistent.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for dal makhani not separately reported in ICMR-NIN 2017 tables; these values are not available from the approved source. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the cooked dish not reported in ICMR-NIN 2017 for this preparation. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN 2017 — no industrial trans fats in this traditional preparation). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — dal makhani is a vegetarian dish with no animal flesh; trace dairy contribution is below detection threshold and reported as 0 in ICMR-NIN 2017). GI is an analogous estimate based on component legume GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for dal makhani as a composite dish was found in the approved GI source. GL per serving calculated on 32g available carbohydrate per 250g serving. Occasion tags in source input ('snack') and ethnic tags ('mamak') appear inconsistent with the dish's identity as a North Indian Punjabi restaurant main course; these have been corrected to reflect the dish's actual cultural context. The dish is served at Indian restaurants in Singapore including Ananda Bhavan and Saravana Bhavan.
